coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are recognized as sensitive probes for the detection of Hg2+ due to their remarkable fluorescent properties and ease of synthesis. However, drawbacks such as high costs, intricate synthesis, and non-portability hinder their broader applications. This study reported the synthesis of tunable multicolor CDs, including blue (B-CDs), green (G-CDs), and orange (O-CDs), by using hydrothermal methods while adjusting precursors and temperature. The observed fluorescence redshift from B-CDs to G-CDs or O-CDs arose from interactions among particle size, sp² conjugation, and surface oxidation, while the redshift from G-CDs to O-CDs was linked to surface fluorophores. Moreover, a ratiometric probe was developed by mixing B-CDs (response signal) and O-CDs (reference signal). Hg2+ significantly quenched B-CDs fluorescence due to static quenching from electron transfer. The probe demonstrated excellent stability and sensitivity, with limits of detection (LOD) of 4.8 nM in the 0–0.6 μM range and 9.2 nM in a broader range (0–50 μM). A portable smartphone-assisted detection platform was further developed by integrating the probe into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) hydrogel. Both methods manifested the capability to accurately quantify Hg2+ in real water samples. This research provides innovative strategies for multicolor CDs and a sensing platform for rapid on-site Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: The rational design of ratiometric probe based on fluorescence carbon dots and its PVA film platform for the detection of Hg2+: Tunable mechanism and the performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are recognized as sensitive probes for the detection of Hg2+ due to their remarkable fluorescent properties and ease of synthesis. However, drawbacks such as high costs, intricate synthesis, and non-portability hinder their broader applications. This study reported the synthesis of tunable multicolor CDs, including blue (B-CDs), green (G-CDs), and orange (O-CDs), by using hydrothermal methods while adjusting precursors and temperature. The observed fluorescence redshift from B-CDs to G-CDs or O-CDs arose from interactions among particle size, sp² conjugation, and surface oxidation, while the redshift from G-CDs to O-CDs was linked to surface fluorophores. Moreover, a ratiometric probe was developed by mixing B-CDs (response signal) and O-CDs (reference signal). Hg2+ significantly quenched B-CDs fluorescence due to static quenching from electron transfer. The probe demonstrated excellent stability and sensitivity, with limits of detection (LOD) of 4.8 nM in the 0–0.6 μM range and 9.2 nM in a broader range (0–50 μM). A portable smartphone-assisted detection platform was further developed by integrating the probe into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) hydrogel. Both methods manifested the capability to accurately quantify Hg2+ in real water samples. This research provides innovative strategies for multicolor CDs and a sensing platform for rapid on-site Hg2+ detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ion (Hg2+) is a significantly cytotoxic carcinogen that is ubiquitously present in the environment due to natural and human activities [1,2]. Of greater concern is the fact that Hg2+ is not naturally biodegradable. Under specific conditions, it can be transformed into a more toxic methylmercury. However, methylmercury has the potential to accumulate in the human body through biomagnification, thereby posing a substantial threat to human health and the environment [3]. To ensure the safety of surface water and food sources, it is imperative to develop a detection method for Hg2+ content with the merit of high accuracy and sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, and user-friendliness.

The fluorescence methods have gained recognition for their ease of use, short response times, low analytical costs, and capacity for visual representation [4]. Among numerous fluorescence materials, carbon dots (CDs) are considered to be the ideal candidate materials for the development of fluorescent probes. They are composed of sp2 hybridized carbon along with various surface functional groups, typically featuring a particle size below 10 nm [5]. The ratiometric fluorescence method combines a response signal with a reference signal, which can be developed by the fluorescent materials emitting at different wavelengths, to detect analytes and establish a reliable internal reference system. Consequently, the method demonstrates high sensitivity and accuracy in detection [6,7].

Up to now, some vital progress has been made in improving the development and application of ratio fluorescence probes based on CDs for Hg2+ detection. For instance, Xie et al., [8]conjugated gold nanoclusters with blue CDs to create a dual-emission ratio probe. Similarly, Guo et al., [9] encapsulated blue CDs within europium metal-organic frameworks to detect Hg2+ in water samples. However, most reported ratio fluorescence probes were composed of blue-green fluorescence CDs with other long-wavelength emitting materials [10]. Adopting two kinds of CDs with distinct fluorescent emissions to construct the probe was still rare. Liu et al. [11] and Li et al. [12] are the sole researchers to have employed this method for the ratiometric detection of Hg2+. Due to the complex interplay of mechanisms underlying the emission from CDs [13], it is challenging to controllably prepare CDs with specific fluorescent emissions and responses [14]. Thus, it is of great importance to concurrently regulate both surface functionalization and fluorescence emission of CDs during the synthesis process while also investigating the mechanisms behind fluorescence changes [15], but these have not attracted much attention.

The solvothermal synthesis of CDs could be an efficient pathway to address the issue because it offers excellent controllability, and the product properties are significantly influenced by the reaction substrates, temperature, solvent, and time [15,16]. Additionally, the selection of reaction substrates is a resultful strategy for the functionalization and fluorescence tuning of CDs [15]. This facilitates us in selecting different precursors based on the characteristics of the ratio probe, thereby achieving controllable fluorescence emission and functionalization of CDs. More importantly, nitrogen and sulfur doping have been revealed to enhance the fluorescence quantum yield and increase the affinity of CDs towards Hg2+ [17,18]. For the other reference signal, the CDs with long-wavelength emission characteristics can be synthesized via a hydrothermal method by using long-wavelength emitting organic fluorescent molecules or organic compounds featuring conjugated structures as precursors [19,20]. Alizarin red may be a suitable choice since it is a long-wavelength-emitting fluorescent dye with a sp2-conjugated structure.

Another challenge in the application of CDs in sensing fields is their aggregation-induced quenching effect. Encapsulating CDs in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films presents an indirect method for creating solid-state CDs [13], which has been shown to prevent aggregation quenching [21]. This approach offers greater potential compared to the conventional studies that involve adsorbing CDs onto test paper, in which the CDs are often in uneven dispersion. However, it is a headache problem to ensure that both two types of CDs are encapsulated in the film during its formation while maintaining the integrity performance of their ratiometric fluorescence. The most useful solution is that the two kinds of CDs are incorporated within PVA films through hydrogen bonds instead of other strong chemical interactions.

Therefore, the blue CDs responsive to Hg2+ in this study were synthesized by using citric acid, polyethyleneimine, and 3-mercaptopropionic acid, while the green and orange CDs were produced by replacing polyethyleneimine with alizarin red. Finally, the ratio probe was constructed by physical mixing of the two CDs and successfully encapsulated them in PVA film for Hg2+ detection. The objectives of this research are to elucidate the mechanism governing CDs' fluorescence emission at the multicolor; evaluate the stability and detection performance of the constructed ratio probe under variable environmental conditions; explore the detection mechanism; develop the portable thin-film fluorescence platform and its detection properties; investigate the practical feasibility of the as-proposed probes and portable platform in real surface water scenarios. Overall, this study could contribute to the synthesis of multicolor CDs, the developed ratio fluorescence probe and associated thin film platform could serve as robust technical tools for real-time on-site Hg2+ analysis in environmental water samples.
The reagents and equipment, fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) measurement, and spiked recovery measurement are described in Text S1-S3 of the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The three CDs were successfully prepared via a one-step solvothermal method. For blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs), 2.0 g citric acid, 0.7 g 3-mercaptopropionic acid, and 0.5 g polyethyleneimine were dissolved in 20 mL purified water. Next, the well-mixed solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor and heated at 160°C for 5 h. After being filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble substances, the filtrate was collected and moved to a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 1000 Da for 48 h to remove the soluble unreacted raw materials and impurities. At last, the purified solution was freeze-dried, and the resulting powder was redissolved in water and preserved at 4°C with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL for follow-up experiments.

Moreover, a similar procedure was applied to prepare green-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) and orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs) with different precursor and hydrothermal conditions. To fabricate G-CDs, 0.5 g citric acid, 0.7 g 3-mercaptopropionic acid, and 0.15 g alizarin red were dissolved in 15 mL ultra-pure water and then heated at 200°C for 7 h. In the case of O-CDs, 0.5 g citric acid, 0.175 g 3-mercaptopropionic acid, and 0.3 g alizarin red were applied to dissolve in 20 mL ultra-pure water and then heated at 160°C for 5 h.
The fluorescence colors of the three CDs were validated through the CIE 1976 chromaticity coordinates (Fig. S1).

7 g PVA particles were added into 100 mL ultra-pure water and violently stirred for 4 h at 90℃ until complete dissolution. 1 g of glycerol was dropped to enhance the optical properties of the film. Then B-CDs and O-CDs were gradually and separately added dropwise to the PVA solution and stirred for 1 h. Next, the two solutions were mixed and stirred for an additional hour before being transferred to a culture dish and air-dried at 25℃ for three days to form the B/O-CDs@PVA film. The resulting film was cut into a rectangular thin film at a size of 0.5×0.5 cm2 uniform dimensions for quenching experiments. A control PVA film was prepared via the same procedure but without the inclusion of any CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance of the as-prepared probe and the film

The ratiometric fluorescent probe to detect C
Hg
ce:section.ce:para:
The ratiometric fluorescent analysis of Hg2+ was conducted at room temperature in ultrapure water. In the typical detection experiments, B-CDs solution (500 μL, 0.2 μg/mL), O-CDs solution (500 μL, 10 μg/mL), Britton-Robinson buffer solution (2 mL, pH 7), and various concentrations of Hg2+ solution (1 mL) were introduced into 10 mL centrifuge tubes. After incubating for 1 min, the mixture was put into the quartz cuvette for fluorescence spectra scanning. The fluorescent spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm with both the excitation and emission slits of 10 nm. At last, the fluorescence ratios of 435 nm and 580 nm (F435/F580) were calculated in the presence of the various concentrations of Hg2+ (C
Hg ), and a linear correlation between them was established to enable the quantitative detection of C
Hg . The samples were collected in triplicate.

Furthermore, the visual measuring processed as follows: the fluorescence colors of the mixture at different C
Hg were recorded under UV light at 365 nm by the professional mode of the HUAWEI Nova 4 smartphone's camera, specifically setting photosensitivity to 1000, shutter speed to 1/6, exposure value to 0, and white balance to 4000 K. Then the color information to RGB values (prime optical colors, R: red, G: green, B: blue) was extracted, in which three points were picked on each sample to calculate the mean value and standard deviation and ensure the accuracy of detection. Finally, the linear curve of R/B values versus C
Hg was built to complete the smartphone-assisted visual detection.

To assess the selectivity of this ratiometric fluorescent probe towards Hg2+, a series of solutions at 0.6 μM were prepared for the experiments. These solutions included individual or dual coexisting systems with Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Bi3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+, as well as common anion such as Cl- (0–5000 mg/L), SO4 2- (0–3000 mg/L), NO3 - (0–20 mg/L), CO3 2- (0–10 mg/L), and PO4 3- (0–10 mg/L). Similarly, the subsequent detection was referred to the abovementioned process, in which each scenario was repeated three times to achieve accurate and reliable results.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
The performance of the B/O-CDs@PVA film to detect C
Hg
ce:section.ce:para:
The thin film was immersed in solutions at varying C
Hg for 1 h; then the fluorescence color of the film was captured to obtain the RGB values; the reliable linear line between the R/B values and the C
Hg could be established.

The surface water samples in Yongjiang River, Nanning City, Guangxi, and drinking water from a water purifier (Midea, JD1750S-RO model) were collected to estimate the practicability and feasibility of the as-established method including the ratiometric fluorescent probe and film sensor. The samples were filtered to remove the impurities through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and then spiked at 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 5, 15, 20, and 40 µM Hg2+. The blank and the spiked concentrations were determined according to the procedure mentioned in Section 2.4.

Three types of fluorescent CDs, labeled B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs, were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal approach ( Fig. 1). The B-CDs were prepared by employing the anhydrous citric acid, mercaptopropionic acid, and polyethyleneimine as precursor materials. As such, the goal of a redshift in emission could be achieved by incorporating long-wavelength-emitting fluorescent dyes as the precursor. Specifically, the alizarin red was adopted to replace the polyethyleneimine to synthesize another two kinds of CDs.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were employed to evaluate the optical characteristics of the three CDs in this investigation. They exhibited a pale yellow color under visible light and emitted blue, green, and orange emissions under UV lamps, respectively. Fig. 2a-c showed that the UV–vis absorption spectra of the three CDs displayed the peaks at 240 nm, 255 nm, and 260 nm, corresponding to the π→π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 domains [22,23]. Generally speaking, a greater absorption wavelength meant the existence of a larger π electron system such as the abundant aromatic rings or conjugated structural units within the molecule [24]. The absorption spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs exhibited prominently identifiable broad absorption peaks in the ultraviolet region at 356 nm, 454 nm, and 421 nm, which was attributable to the n→π* transitions of surface functional groups [10]. Furthermore, the G-CDs and O-CDs may own similar surface states [25] since their adjacency absorption peaks in the ultraviolet region.

As illustrated in Fig. 2d-f, B-CDs showcased excitation-dependent luminescence behavior, inversely, G-CDs and O-CDs exhibited excitation-independent luminescence characteristics [26,27]. The maximum emission peaks of the three CDs were observed at 435 nm, 499 nm, and 580 nm, respectively, and corresponding to optimal excitation wavelengths of 370 nm, 400 nm, and 460 nm. It was noteworthy that G-CDs presented dual emission peaks and the optimal excitation wavelength was determined by the peak within the green spectral range. The 3D spectra (Fig. 2g-i) of these CDs agreed with the aforementioned analysis. The B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs showed high fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of 25.18 %, 26.48 %, and 14.96 % in aqueous solution with quinine sulfate (QYs 54 %) as the reference standard.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The redshift mechanism in the fluorescence emission among CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Diverse characterization techniques were employed to elucidate the mechanism behind the redshift in fluorescence emission of the three CDs in this investigation.

The TEM and Nano Measurer 1.2 were engaged to reveal the morphology and the size distribution of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs. Fig. 3a-f illustrated that the B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs were all well-dispersed, uniform spherical particles. The average diameters were approximately 1.75 nm for B-CDs, 4.33 nm for G-CDs, and 1.81 nm for O-CDs. The results demonstrated that the quantum size effect might be the main reason for the redshift in the fluorescence emission wavelength from B-CDs to G-CDs or O-CDs [28]: the increasing particle size of CDs led to the fluorescence emission redshift [29]. The TEM images in Fig. 3a-c (inset) displayed that the B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs had clear lattice structures with 0.21 nm crystal spacing, which corresponded to the (100) planes of graphite carbon [30,31]. This observation suggested that the three CDs possessed highly crystalline carbon structures.

The XRD patterns were utilized to assess the degree of graphitization in these CDs. Fig. 3g exhibited the O-CDs had two peaks at 21.2° and 26.3° that were assigned to the (002) plane of the graphite structure. The broad peak at 21.2° exhibited a diffraction angle lower than that of the standard graphitic (002) crystal plane, which is 26.5°. This reduction in angle contributed to an enlarged interlayer distance, originating from the steric hindrance of functional groups on the graphene edge or the plan distortion from sp3 C in the graphene plane [10,32,33]. The peak of O-CDs at 26.3° was close to the characteristic (002) diffraction peak of graphite, which indicated that the internal structure of O-CDs was mainly formed by the dense accumulation of highly conjugated sp2 domain [33]. While the B-CDs and G-CDs showed only one significant peak at 21.2°, suggesting a lower degree of graphitization [34]. The effective conjugation length theory pointed out that a longer effective conjugation length signified higher graphitization levels and a corresponding redshift in emission [35]. Hence, the observed redshift of the fluorescence emission wavelength among the three CDs can be attributed to the increased degree of graphitization.

The FT-IR spectra of the three CDs in Fig. 3h displayed several similar absorption peaks in the range of 400–4000 cm−1, indicating that they had similar surface chemical groups. To be specific, the series characteristic peaks at 3549 cm−1, 3477 cm−1, and 3412 cm−1 were due to N-H/O-H stretching vibration [36]. The peaks located at 1637 and 1618 cm−1, 1263 cm−1, and 1134 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of CO, C-O, and C-S [17,37,38]. Nevertheless, some minor differences should be mentioned: the CC stretching vibration peak of G-CDs and O-CDs was discovered at 1475 cm−1 [39], which was attributed to the benzene ring of precursor alizarin red, implying that the presence of conjugated aromatic structure [25,40], so was stretching vibration peak of -SO3 - at 1041 cm−1. The B-CDs owned a unique absorption at 1388 cm−1, which was assigned to the stretching vibration of the C-N from polyethyleneimine [23].

The results emerging from Fig. 3i depicted the three CDs were dominantly composed of C (284.8 eV), O (532.1 eV), and S (164.1 eV). Additionally, the B-CDs exhibited N (400.1 eV) content attributable to the incorporation of polyethylenimine. Fig. 4 displays the high-resolution XPS spectra for the C 1 s, O 1 s, and S 2p orbitals of these CDs. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. 4a-c) demonstrated three discernable deconvolution peaks at binding energies of 284.8, 286.1, and 288.5 eV, corresponding to sp2 C (CC/C-C groups), sp3 C (C-N/C-S/C-O groups), and CO functional group, respectively [41]. The analysis of the O1s spectra (Fig. 4d-f) indicated the existence of CO (531.1 eV) and C-OH (532.3 eV), the percentage ratio was of great significance. The C-OH contributed to 59.81 % of the total mass of the B-CDs, whereas the CO occupied the majority mass ratio (75.4 %, 55.23 %) of G-CDs and O-CDs.

In the high-resolution of S 2p spectra, the curves of G-CDs and O-CDs were split into three discernible peaks belonging to the C-S at 162.8 eV, CS/C-SOx at 167.1 eV, and C-SOx at 168.1 eV (Fig. 4g-i) with different mass ratio [42]. It was worth mentioning that the B-CDs exhibited only one peak derived from the C-S bond at 162.8 eV, but it contained C-N and N-H groups at binding energies of 400.2 and 402.7 eV in the high-resolution N 1 s spectra (Fig. S2). These results were in alignment with the FTIR analysis, which indicated that the surfaces of all three CDs contained substantial functional groups originating from the precursors.

The weight percentage content of each element or chemical bond was calculated to further explore the redshift mechanism (Table S1-5). The total O/C ratios of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs were determined as 0.23, 0.44, and 0.31, indicating a greater surface oxidation degree in G-CDs and O-CDs compared to B-CDs. According to the surface state theory of CDs fluorescence emission [41], the increased surface oxidation of the CDs could lead to the observable redshift. Meanwhile, the sp2 C (CC/C-C) accounted for 46.85 %, 76.13 %, and 73.43 % of the total mass in the C 1 s spectra of the three CDs. The significantly higher values of the sp2 C could be another reason for the redshift based on the effective conjugation length theory [35].

In summary, the redshift of fluorescence emission wavelength from B-CDs to G-CDs or O-CDs was dominantly ascribed to the larger particle size, abundant sp2 C content, and higher surface oxidation degree of CDs. However, the redshift of fluorescence emission wavelength from G-CDs to O-CDs did not conform to the three theories because of the insignificant phenomena. Therefore, it was speculated that the redshift was attributed to the molecular state theory [43], that is, the fluorescence of G-CDs and O-CDs mainly came from the fluorophores attached to the surface.

To be specific, both G-CDs and O-CDs were synthesized from identical precursors (alizarin red, citric acid, and mercaptopropionic acid) at varying quality ratios and hydrothermal treatment temperatures. Alizarin red serves as a long-wavelength emission dye, which incorporates a C-SO3 functional group within its molecular structure. The C-SOx in the S 2p spectrum can be associated with alizarin red [44]. Unsurprisingly, its proportion in O-CDs was 83.11 %, surpassing the 42.6 % observed in G-CDs. This disparity suggested that the surface of O-CDs probably possessed a greater abundance of fluorophores originating from alizarin red on their surfaces compared to G-CDs, thereby leading to the pronounced redshift in the emission wavelength of O-CDs. It was in agreement with the molecular state theory.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The construct of ratiometric fluorescent probe B/O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The response effect of the three CDs towards Hg2+ was tested to explore their potential for chemical sensing. As shown in Fig. S3a-c and inset, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs could be significantly quenched by various C
Hg . By contrast, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs and O-CDs remained unaffected by increasing levels of C
Hg . Consequently, ratiometric fluorescent probes could be designed based on B-CDs and G-CDs or B-CDs and O-CDs for the discernment of C
Hg . In this case, B-CDs could serve as the response signal, while the other CDs would act as the reference signals.

Therefore, the B-CDs and G-CDs were firstly well-mixed, and then the various levels (0–25 μM) Hg2+ were introduced. The outcomes indicated that the corresponding fluorescence peaks of B-CDs and G-CDs decreased to varying extents simultaneously, and no evident change in the fluorescence color of the mixed CDs was observed under UV light (365 nm) (Fig. S3d and inset). The B-CDs and G-CDs had adjacent emission wavelengths (see Fig. S3a-b), which could interfere with each other. This was the fundamental reason for this phenomenon. It was confirmed that the ratiometric fluorescent probe B/G-CDs was ineffective.

Whereafter, the alternative ratiometric fluorescent probe B/O-CDs exhibited a different trend: the peak at 435 nm of the B-CDs was regularly quenched and the peak at 580 nm of the O-CDs remained unchanged along with the various C
Hg . Another interesting phenomenon was that the fluorescence color of the hybrid system showed a conspicuous alteration under UV light (365 nm) from blue-violet to pink-orange (Fig. S3e and inset). That performance was satisfied with the requirement of a ratiometric fluorescent probe, declaring its successful construction. Furthermore, the B/O-CDs prepared by physical mixing of B-CDs and O-CDs showed promise as a potential probe for the visual detection of C
Hg . Fig. S3f displays the fluorescence spectra of B/O-CDs at various excitation wavelengths, which were highly dependent on the two CDs. The optimal excitation wavelength of the B/O-CDs was determined by the B-CDs due to their leading role as the response signal. B-CDs exhibited a pronounced and sharp emission peak under ultraviolet excitation at 370 nm. Hence, 370 nm was chosen as the optimal excitation wavelength for the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical stability of ratiometric fluorescent probe B/O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent stability played a pivotal role in the sensing performance and practical application of the sensors. As such, the fluorescence emission characteristics were evaluated under varying conditions, including different pHs, the concentration of NaCl, the exposure to ultraviolet irradiation and sunlight.

The fluorescence intensity of B/O-CDs along with the pH is illustrated in Fig. S4a-b. The as-constructed probe owned blue and orange fluorescence emission peaks simultaneously within pH 6–9 without any significant change in peak position, thereby ensuring the applicability in natural water environments. The intensity exhibited an observable decline when the pH increased from 6 to 9, but it did not impact the response of the system to the C
Hg as shown in Fig. S5b. However, it was observed that both B-CDs and O-CDs exhibited blue or redshifts under acidic or alkaline conditions (Fig. S4b). This resulted in the almost disappearance of the orange fluorescence emission at pH 2–5 and 10–13. The phenomena could potentially be ascribed to the varying pHs, especially the acidic or alkaline conditions that influenced the surface state density and energy level structure of B-CDs and O-CDs, ultimately altering their luminescent properties [17,45].

As illustrated in Fig. S4c, the F435/F580 values of the probe remained invariable with the elevated concentration of NaCl up to 4 M, the negligible influence might be attributed to the substantial adaptability of the ionic strength exhibited by B/O-CDs, resulting in changeless fluorescence emission under elevated NaCl concentrations [2]. This finding revealed the exceptional salt tolerance of B/O-CDs in physiological fields.

Moreover, the as-prepared fluorescent probe possessed a stable intensity (Fig. S4d-e) even when exposed to UV light for 45 min or sunlight for 90 min, indicating its exceptional stability and resistance to photobleaching. More interestingly, the F435/F580 values remained essentially constant when the probe was preserved at 4℃ for 30 days (Fig. S4f). This stability could be attributed to the protective effect of the surface functional groups of B/O-CDs [17], indicating their favorable optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
The detection performance of B/O-CDs to C
Hg

ce:section.ce:para: The optimization of the pH value and incubation time was necessary to enhance the response of B/O-CDs towards Hg2+. Given the dual emission peaks of B/O-CDs within the pH range of 6–9 (Fig. S4a-b), the sensing performance was evaluated by the F435/F580 value at 0.5 μM Hg2+. The surface of B-CDs exhibited a negative charge in the pH range of 6–9 since its zero point charge was determined as 5.18 (Fig. S5a), which was beneficial to the attraction of Hg2+ in natural water environments. Fig. S5b pointed out that a dramatic decline trend was found in F435/F580 when the pH increased from 6 to 8, whereas the quenching efficiency first climbed up, peaked at pH 7, and then declined. These results could be ascribed to the surface-charged condition of the CDs. To be specific, the -NH2 on the surface of B-CDs was protonated to form positively charged -NH3 + in an acidic environment, thereby weakening the interaction between B-CDs and Hg2+ and diminishing quenching efficiency [45]. Under alkaline conditions, Hg2+ was easily hydrolyzed and might combine with -OH, resulting in less Hg2+ that can interact with B-CDs, which would also reduce the degree of quenching [46]. The results suggested that pH 7 was the suitable pH for subsequent experiments.

On the other hand, the incubation time was also an imperative parameter for the detection of Hg2+. As illustrated in Fig. S5c, the F435/F580 value rapidly decreased and stabilized within 1 min with the addition of 0.5 μM Hg2+ to the B/O-CDs, manifesting that the probe B/O-CDs could achieve rapid and stable response to Hg2+. Hence, 1 min was enough for the fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensitivity of the B/O-CDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
A series of various concentrations of Hg2+ were spiked to B/O-CDs solution under optimized reaction parameters (pH 7, reaction for 1 min) to further explore the quantitative analysis of B/O-CDs to Hg2+. The fluorescence spectra of B/O-CDs were recorded in Fig. 5a. A corresponding decrease was discovered in the fluorescence peak at 435 nm assigning to the B-CDs, while no obvious change at 580 nm ascribing to O-CDs when the C
Hg was elevated from 0 μM to 50 μM. This reflected the typical feature of the ratiometric fluorescent probe B/O-CDs: sensitive responsive CDs integrated with reference CDs.

The F435/F580 was calculated according to the fluorescence intensity ratio at 435 nm and 580 nm, it dramatically declined and then gradually flattened at a certain value along with the augment of C
Hg (Fig. 5b). Interestingly, the trend was consistent with a logarithmic function.

Therefore, the curve could be described in two distinct ways: a low concentration region (0＜C
Hg ≤0.6 μM) and a wider concentration range (0＜C
Hg ≤50 μM) as depicted in Fig. 5b. For the part of low C
Hg (Fig. 5c), a linear equation of y=−3.8640x+9.4878 was fitted between the F435/F580 and 0–0.6 μM Hg2+ with a higher R2 of 0.997. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 4.8 nM by utilizing the formula 3σ/k (where σ was the standard deviation from the blank solution during nine measurements and k was the slope of the calibration curve), which was found to be lower than the maximum concentration of Hg2+ in drinking water regulated by the WHO (30 nM) [47]. In the whole concentration range of 0–50 μM (Fig. 5d), another remarkable linearity between F435/F580 and LogC
Hg was achieved according to the equation of F435/F580 = −2.0061 LogC
Hg +7.0596 (R2=0.998), resulting in an LOD of 9.2 nM. Therefore, it could be summarized that the B/O-CDs not only exhibited high sensitivity in the low range of 0–0.6 μM but also still showed excellent sensitivity across a wider range of up to 50 μM. This finding fully manifested its great potential in detecting trace and micro-levels of Hg2+.

Moreover, the detection performance of the synthesized B/O-CDs and state-of-the-art fluorescent probes are listed in Table S6. Previously reported probes, such as dual-emission CDs [48], had a broad linear range (0.5–50 μM) with the LOD of 37 nM. In contrast, high-sensitivity ratio probes, like P-R-CDs [12] and b/r-CDs [11], demonstrated limited ranges (0.005–0.1 μM and 0.01–10 μM, respectively). They had struggled to concurrently attain both an extensive linear range and low LOD. Noteworthy, the ratio probe developed in this study exhibited both a low LOD and an extended linear range, enhancing sensitivity for real water sample analysis and broadening its application potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity and anti-interference of the detection system
ce:section.ce:para: Taking into account the intricate nature of actual environmental water samples, the selectivity and anti-interference capabilities of the ratiometric probe were assessed under coexisting common anion and cations conditions (pH 7, reaction for 1 min). Consequently, 18 kinds of metal ions at 0.6 μM and anion (Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 -, CO3 2-, PO4 3-) at the different levels were selected to compose individual and dual coexisting systems to test the selectivity toward Hg2+.

As illustrated in Fig. S6a, both Hg2+ and Cu2+ in the individual system exhibited fluorescence quenching of B-CDs, of which Hg2+ showed a more pronounced quenching effect than Cu2+. This observation indicated that the existence of Cu2+ would interfere with the detection of Hg2+. The response of B-CDs to Cu2+ can be attributed to these issues: Firstly, both Hg2+ and Cu2+ exist in the same oxidation state and ionic radii, which allows them to display similar stability and behavior characteristics including comparable coordination ability and reactivity; Secondly, Cu2+ exhibits a strong affinity for binding to -NH2 groups [49], which are present in the surface functional groups of B-CDs. Consequently, the presence of Cu2+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of B-CDs. While different results occurred in the dual coexisting system: the presence of other metal ions, including Cu2+, did not affect the response of B-CDs to Hg2+ (Fig. S6b). The influence of Cu2+ was not found in this situation, which was potentially due to the higher affinity and faster reaction kinetics of B-CDs toward Hg2+ rather than Cu2+ [45].

Besides, Fig. 5e presents the (F/F0)435 nm values of other metal ions in response to B-CDs that were close to 1 in the individual system except for Hg2+ and Cu2+. B-CDs exhibited the most pronounced response to Hg2+ with (F/F0)435 nm of 68.95 %, followed by a response to Cu2+ with (F/F0)435 nm of 79.48 %. In the dual coexisting system, the presence of additional metal ions, including Cu2+, did not impact the responsiveness of B-CDs to Hg2+ due to its (F/F0)435 nm remained roughly 70 %, which was nearly equivalent to that observed in the presence of Hg2+ alone. The identical results were observed when the ratio response was expressed as F435/F580 in Fig. S6c. Hence it could be concluded that the detection of Hg2+ by B/O-CDs remained unaffected in the presence of metal ions.

On the other hand, the F435/F580 value of B/O-CDs was unaffected by increasing concentrations of SO4 2- (0–3000 mg/L), NO3 - (0–20 mg/L), CO3 2- (0–10 mg/L), and PO4 3- (0–10 mg/L), revealing the anti-interference of the detection system (Fig. S7a-d). Whereas a mass of Cl- did impact the detection: the F435/F580 began to rise once the Cl- increased to 400 mg/L; and the quenching did not occur when the Cl- content was higher than 5000 mg/L (Fig. S7e). The primary reason for these phenomena was the high content of Cl-, which produced strong ionic strength at the trace level of Hg2+ (0.4 μM); additionally, the chelation of Cl- and Hg2+ would occupy much more approachable sites of B-CDs, which led to the weakening of the F435/F580 to some extent. Furthermore, it was reported that the level of Cl- in the freshwater was smaller than 200 mg/L, which would not impede the detection process.

Those aforementioned results could reveal advantageously that the constructed fluorescent ratiometric probe possessed excellent selectivity, anti-interference properties, and great promise for practical application in real fresh water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The establishment of the smartphone-assisted visual analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
A striking result emerging from Fig. 6a was that the probe manifested a conspicuous alteration in color under UV light (365 nm) as the elevated C
Hg from 0 to 10 μM. Therefore, the B/O-CDs were capable of realizing the visual detection of Hg2+. The color information was quantified via CIE coordinates to assess changes with increasing C
Hg . The scatter diagram in Fig. 6b stayed in a line, which proved the great potential of B/O-CDs in establishing a visual sensing platform for Hg2+ detection.

Based on this, the visual detection of Hg2+ was explored as follows: initially, the fluorescence images of B/O-CDs along with different C
Hg (0–10 μM) were captured by a smartphone camera (HUAWEI Nova 4) under a portable UV lamp in a dark environment. Subsequently, the RGB values of the images were extracted by using the Color Identification app on the mobile phone, where R, G, and B denoted red, green, and blue channels, correspondingly. The color data in Table S7 indicated that the B and G values that derived from B-CDs decreased along with the increasing C
Hg , while the R-values that originated from O-CDs remained relatively stable. Hence, the linear relationship was fitted with the ratios of B/R (Fig. S8a), R/B (Fig. 6c), and (B+G)/R (Fig. S8b) versus C
Hg . Finally, the R/B ratio displayed the most favorable linear relationship with C
Hg in the range of 0–10 μM because of the highest R2 of 0.994 with a linear regression equation of y=0.0265x+0.6336. The LOD of this method was as low as 370 nM. The aforementioned results fully manifested that the designed smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on B/O-CDs enabled rapid and highly sensitive visual detection of Hg2+, which met the requirements of on-site tests for environmental emergency pollution events.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection performance stability of the B/O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the reliability and stability of the B/O-CDs, both intra-day and inter-day precision experiments were conducted at C
Hg of 0.1 and 0.4 μM. The intra-day precision was determined by measuring the fluorescent responses of B/O-CDs six times within a single day, while the inter-day precision was evaluated by conducting daily tests for six consecutive days. As illustrated in Fig. S9, it was evident that a negligible change in F435/F580 was found over 24-hour periods and across six consecutive days. The relative standard deviation (RSD) values of the results were 0.51 % and 0.48 % for intra-day precision at 0.1 and 0.4 μM Hg2+; and that for inter-day precision were 0.25 % and 0.33 %. These minimal RSD values (below 1 %) proved the outstanding precision and stability of the B/O-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection performance of B/O-CDs@PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: In an effort to broaden the application scope of B/O-CDs, a uniformly dispersed B/O-CDs@PVA composite film was fabricated. It not only enhanced the portability but also effectively prevented fluorescence quenching resulting from the aggregation of CDs in solution. Fig. 7a depicts a visual representation showcasing the PVA film and B/O-CDs@PVA film when they were exposed to sunlight and UV light. The fluorescence intensity of the B/O-CDs@PVA film was markedly superior to that of the PVA film, thereby providing preliminary evidence that the CDs were effectively encapsulated within the PVA film. Upon the incorporation of CDs into the PVA film, the hybrid film owned a noticeable blueshift in peak (1114.5 cm−1) belonging to the C-O stretching vibration within the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 7b). Their almost identical FTIR curves provided evidence for the formation of hydrogen bonds between the CDs and PVA chains [50], since no emerging functional groups were discovered. This enabled an optimal fluorescence behavior in the B/O-CDs@PVA film.

So the detection sensitivity of the film sensor was further studied. After immersing the film in solution at 0–100 μM C
Hg , the fluorescence color of the film varied from blue to orange (Fig. 7c) under 365 nm UV light, the phenomenon was aligned with the color variation trend exhibited by the liquid probe in Section 3.5.4. It shifted to a bright green at the C
Hg of 250 μM, indicating that the film sensor had a special fluorescence color response toward a high level of Hg2+. The good readability under the UV lamp and visual semi-quantitative detection by the naked eye endowed this sensor with great promising appliable possibility. Utilizing the superior resolution of smartphone cameras instead of the naked eye, the RGB values of the image were extracted to achieve quantitative visual detection of Hg2+. The procedures for detection were the same as those for the liquid visual probe.

Table S8 presents the corresponding average RGB values of the film sensor across the range of 0–250 μM, the B-values gradually declined while the R-values elevated when the C
Hg varied from 0 to 60 µM. Hence, the R/G ratio values increased linearly with the incremental C
Hg , following the formula of y=0.00484x+0.00615 with a high R2 of 0.998. The LOD of this method was calculated as 594 nM.

These findings demonstrated that the smartphone-assisted B/O-CDs@PVA film sensor platform can be effectively utilized for quantitative analysis of C
Hg and featured with portability, intelligence, and visualization. This method not only offered a straightforward and efficient approach for C
Hg monitoring but also sparked ideas for the advancement of detection methodologies, showcasing great potential for on-site point-of-care testing in the future.

The fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, O-CDs, B/O-CDs, B-CDs+Hg2+, O-CDs+Hg2+, and B/O-CDs+Hg2+ are displayed in Fig. 8a. The presence of Hg2+ led to a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs, whereas the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs kept unaffected. The same phenomena were found in the B/O-CDs at the emission peak of 435 nm derived from B-CDs and that of 580 nm from O-CDs. This observation confirmed that the B-CDs served as the response signal and O-CDs as the reference signal.

To further investigate the detection mechanism, the measurements of absorption spectra, zeta potentials, and fluorescence lifetimes were carried out. As shown in Fig. 8b, there was no overlap between the absorption spectrum of Hg2+ and the emission or excitation spectra of B-CDs, which indicated that the quenching mechanism was not due to either the inner filter effect or forster resonance energy transfer [51]. It was noteworthy that the characteristic absorption peak of B-CDs at 241 nm was inconspicuous in the presence of Hg2+ (Fig. 8c), suggesting the generation of new nonfluorescent species due to static quenching [52].

What’s more, Fig. 8d-e illustrated that no significant differences were observed in the average fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs (9.417 ns) and B-CDs+Hg2+ (9.329 ns), and neither was B/O-CDs (9.667 ns) or B/O-CDs+Hg2+ (9.624 ns). These results confirmed that the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs by Hg2+ was a static quenching process [53,54]. Meanwhile, the zeta potential of B-CDs and B/O-CDs varied from −10.66 mV to −4.40 mV and −46.81 mV to −27.06 mV (Fig. 8f) with the introduction of Hg2+. This revealed the possibility of electron transfer between B-CDs and Hg2+ [45].

Therefore, it could be concluded that the detection of Hg2+ was a static quenching process accompanied by electron transfer between B-CDs and Hg2+, in which the B-CDs served as the response signal and O-CDs as the reference signal.

The real water spiked experiments were explored to verify the practicability and feasibility of the B/O-CDs for the detection of Hg2+. After being spiked with a certain amount of Hg2+, the samples were directly analyzed by the as-reported ratiometric fluorescent probe for quantification without complicated pretreatment, and the C
Hg in the spiked samples was measured by the atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) for comparison. As listed in Table 1, the deviations between the values measured by the AFS and the B/O-CDs or the B/O-CDs@PVA ranged within 0.57–11.2 %, which confirmed the accuracy of the proposed method was comparable to the standard method. Furthermore, the spiked recoveries of B/O-CDs varied in the range of 94.00 %-103.0 % with an RSD of 0.57 %-9.79 %. In comparison, the film sensors detected 96.85 %-104.1 % of the spiked content with an RSD of 8.22 %-11.2 %. Both of the two methods could be capable of accurately quantifying C
Hg in real water samples, their only difference was the linear detection range and detection limit. Specifically, the method based on the B/O-CDs solution was suitable for the trace level detection of the Hg2+, the other method integrated with visual B/O-CDs@PVA film was appropriate for the C
Hg at ppm level. Hence, the developed methods based on the B/O-CDs possess high accuracy, portability, and strong application potential. They can satisfy not only the daily analytical requirement but also the environmental emergency events.

In this study, three fluorescent CDs emitting blue, green, and orange colors were controllably synthesized through a hydrothermal process by varying the precursor and temperature. The redshift in fluorescence emission from blue to green or orange CDs was attributed to the combined effects of quantum size, conjugation length, and surface states, while the shift from green to orange CDs was due to molecular states. Those CDs were well-dispersed uniform spherical particles with average diameters of 1.75–4.33 nm and highly crystalline carbon structure. They also contained substantial functional groups such as CO, C-S, and -NH2. A ratiometric fluorescent probe was prepared by mixing the B-CDs (the response signal) and O-CDs (the reference signal) in proportion, which possessed strong stability under various conditions such as pHs, ion strength, and UV or sunlight exposure. Upon the optimal detection conditions (pH 7, reaction for 1 min), the probe not only exhibited a high linear response with a low LOD of 4.8 nM for the C
Hg of 0–0.6 µM but also owned excellent linearity over a wider range of 0–50 µM with the LOD of 9.2 nM. In addition, a B/O-CDs@PVA thin film sensor was successfully fabricated to address the issues related to CDs release and the loss of quenching effect. By integrating the film with a smartphone to aid in the real-time analysis of color parameters, the portable platform was capable of quantitative detection C
Hg of 0–60 µM at the LOD of 594 nM. The detection of C
Hg involved a static quenching process with electron transfer between B-CDs and Hg2+. Therefore, this study not only provided insights into the theoretical elucidation of the luminescence mechanism of CDs and the synthesis of multicolor CDs but also presented a technical strategy for portable and visually accessible on-site detection of Hg2+ in real water samples.
